name: chole masala
instructions:
The darker, thicker, more aromatic chickpea preparation. Distinguished from chana masala by black tea in the bean-cook, whole spices (cardamom + cloves + cinnamon), and the anardana/tea/cardamom floral wash. The reddit reference recipe at the bottom of the notes is the primary source.

SHARED SPICE BASE: cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, kashmiri chili powder, cardamom, plus kasoori methi at the finish. NO amchur — this recipe's tang comes from the anardana in the spice mix and the black tea's tannin; adding amchur on top pushes the dish into overly-floral territory (see failure modes).

DISTINCTIVES:
- Chickpeas as the legume. Kabuli chana is the western-supermarket default; kala chana is the indian-grocery specialty variant (see note below).
- ONIONS: BOILED (3-4 min in water) then BLENDED into a smooth puree. Non-negotiable — pureeing raw onions is bitter and aggressive.
- WHOLE SPICES bloomed in ghee/oil at the start: bay leaf, green cardamom pods, cloves, cinnamon stick.
- BLACK TEA in the bean-cooking liquid — this is the chole color/tannin signature. Cook the chickpeas in water with a tea bag + bay + cardamom + cloves + cinnamon, then reserve the spiced stock for the gravy.
- ASAFETIDA in the ground spice mix (a pinch).
- FRESH TOMATOES only, pureed. NO tomato paste (that's rajma).
- GHEE (or ghee + oil combination — the reddit recipe favors this).
- Cilantro finish; kasoori methi non-optional.
- Mint welcome alongside cloves when the tea is doing its signature work — see mint-and-cloves note below.

PROCEDURE (SUMMARY — SEE FULL REDDIT REFERENCE RECIPE AT THE BOTTOM):
1. Cook chickpeas in water with tea bag + bay + cardamom + cloves + cinnamon; reserve the spiced stock.
2. Boil onions 3-4 min, then puree smooth. Puree tomatoes separately. Make a ginger-garlic paste.
3. Heat ghee (or ghee + oil). Bloom the whole spices briefly.
4. Add pureed boiled onions; cook 15-20 min until light brown. Add ginger-garlic paste and green chilies; cook a couple of minutes.
5. Add pureed tomatoes; cook 15 min stirring. Add ground spice mix; cook 3-4 min.
6. Add chickpeas; cook a couple of minutes. Add reserved tea-spiced stock and salt; simmer 5-7 min.
7. Optional tempering finish (ghee + garlic + kashmiri chili + cumin + ginger juliennes, poured over).
8. Sprinkle cilantro; add mint if going that path.
notes:
This recipe used to be Variant A of a master-tomato-dal file. Now standalone.

CHOLE VS CHANA MASALA — WHY THEY'RE SEPARATE RECIPES: chole and chana masala are both chickpea preparations but represent genuinely different dishes. Chole (this recipe) leans DARKER, THICKER, more whole-spice-and-tea forward — black tea, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, sometimes anardana for tartness. Chana masala (see chana-masala) leans LIGHTER, TANGIER, and lemon-zest-forward (no amchur there either), skips the tea, more visible tomato, peppers welcome, mint and rosemary at the finish. In indian home cooking these are often blurred, but the axes above are the real distinction. Don't try to hedge.

CHOLE AND CHANA ALSO REFER TO DIFFERENT CHICKPEA VARIETIES: kabuli chana (large beige, what western supermarkets stock) vs kala chana (small dark brown/black, more common in indian grocery stores). Each has its own preferred dish style. Since I default to kabuli chana (regular supermarket), that legume-level distinction isn't captured. Worth knowing it exists.

=== ONION TREATMENT — BOILED AND BLENDED ===

BOIL BEFORE BLENDING — NON-NEGOTIABLE: pre-boil the onions in water for 3-4 min before pureeing — pureeing raw onion is bitter and aggressive.

WHY BLEND AT ALL? two reasons:
- CHICKPEAS CARRY THE TEXTURE: the beans themselves are the main structural texture of chole. Coarse onions compete with the beans for textural attention — you'd end up with a chunky-everything dish where nothing leads. Smooth onion base stays out of the bean's way.
- CHOLE'S BLACK TEA NEEDS BALANCING: the unflavored black tea brings a fruity/floral character. Blended boiled onions (and a pinch of asafetida) give the masala a denser, sweeter aromatic base that absorbs the floral edge. Chunky onions leave the tea + cardamom floating on top.

=== MINT AND CLOVES (BLACK TEA PATH) ===

MINT AND CLOVES IN CHOLE: mint and cloves are both welcome additions when the black tea is doing its signature work. This is a real application of the mint-and-tomato nuance from the herbs and greens article: mint clashes with tomato in simple aromatic dishes, but works in heavily-spiced ones. The chole spice base (cumin, coriander, garam masala, cardamom, kashmiri chili powder, methi, cloves, cinnamon) is exactly the heavy-spice-cabinet context the article describes — enough structural spice work to absorb mint's edge, and the tea+cardamom floral wash actually pairs with mint rather than fighting it. Cloves bridge the tea, cardamom, and mint into a cohesive whole. Both go in toward the end so they don't get cooked into oblivion.

=== GREEN PEPPERS — STRONG AFFINITY FOR SPICY ONES ===

Chole has a strong affinity for spicy green peppers — poblanos, jalapeños, and serranos all belong here. The dark tea + cardamom + cloves + cinnamon spice base is dense enough to hold up to real heat, and the peppers' green-vegetal character punches through the concentrated masala in a way that lifts the dish. Reach for spicier over milder — this is the variant that most rewards a hot batch. Anaheim and bell peppers are less at home here (they're milder and read too vegetal against the darker spice stack) — save those for mediterranean dal. Green chilies also show up in the reddit reference recipe as canonical.

Mediterranean dal shares this pepper affinity but tolerates the milder ones (anaheim, bell) better than chole does. Chana masala and rajma DON'T take green peppers well — see those recipes for context.

=== FAILURE MODES ===

TURMERIC IN THE TEA BROTH AS WELL AS IN THE PAN MAKES THE DISH TOO SPICY: turmeric belongs in the gravy/pan spice mix, not in the bean-cooking tea broth. Doubling it up (broth AND pan) compounds the heat and bitterness, especially when you've got jalapeños or other green chiles in the dish.

AMCHUR IS OMITTED FROM THIS RECIPE FOR THE FLORAL-STACK REASON: amchur + tea + cardamom is a triple-floral stack that pushes the dish past what the blended boiled onions and asafetida can absorb. The floral wash starts to dominate and the chole signature reads perfumed rather than savory. This is why chole-masala drops amchur entirely — the tea and cardamom are the two floral notes the dish is built around, and adding a third breaks the balance. Amchur lives in mediterranean-dal and rajma (see those recipes) where there's no tea and no cardamom-heavy structure to fight it.

NO LEMON (JUICE OR ZEST) — SAME FLORAL-STACK PROBLEM: lemon zest was previously listed as a secondary brightness option here, but that's out. Lemon + tea + cardamom is a triple-floral stack problem in the same way amchur + tea + cardamom is — the citrus oils on top of the tea's tannin and cardamom's floral character over-perfume the dish. Chole doesn't want a citrus lift; its brightness comes from the tea's tannin and (if using the reddit reference recipe) the anardana's pomegranate-sweet-tart. Lemon and lemon zest both clashing. If you want lemon-forward chickpeas, make chana masala.

=== SHARED FAMILY RULES ===

KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER: color and gentle heat without overpowering. Different from generic chili powder. Non-optional across the tomato-dal family (chole, chana masala, mediterranean dal, rajma).

KASOORI METHI: non-optional in this family. Load-bearing flavor, not a garnish. Present in both chole masala AND chana masala at the finish.

MINT AT THE FINISH — CANONICAL IN BOTH CHICKPEA VARIANTS: fresh mint stirred in raw at the end works in both chole masala and chana masala. Here in chole, mint pairs with cloves and the tea+cardamom floral wash (see mint-and-cloves note above). In chana masala it sits with rosemary + cilantro + lemon zest instead. Different rationale each variant, same finishing move.

SIBLING RECIPES:
- chana-masala: the lighter, tangier, lemon-and-peppers-forward chickpea preparation. Same legume, different character.
- mediterranean-dal: the chunky lentil dal — dry lentils into the pot, lots of chunky peppers and onions. The most 'curry' of the family.
- rajma: the kidney bean gravy — tomato paste concentrated, ghee non-optional, methi-forward.

=== REFERENCE RECIPE — REDDITOR CHOLE (PRIMARY SOURCE FOR THIS RECIPE) ===

A chana masala recipe found on reddit that's actually chole-shaped (tea + cardamom + cloves + cinnamon in the bean broth). This is the primary reference for chole. Captured in full.

FOR COOKING THE BEANS:
- 1 cup dried garbanzo beans (or 2 cans)
- 1 tsp tea or 1 tea bag
- 2 black cardamom pods, cracked open slightly
- 3 cloves
- ~1 inch cinnamon
- 2 bay leaves
- 1/8 tsp baking soda (skip if using canned)
- salt

FOR THE GRAVY:
- 3-4 tbsp oil/ghee (a combination is best)
- 2 medium onions (red/white/yellow all fine)
- 3 tomatoes
- 6 garlic cloves
- 1 inch ginger
- 2 green chilies

DRY SPICE MIX (mix all in a bowl, set aside) — author uses BOTH a custom mix AND a packaged chana masala blend; he insists this redundancy works better:
- 1 tsp red chili powder
- 1/2 tsp turmeric
- 1 tsp cumin powder
- 1 tsp coriander powder
- 1 tsp garam masala
- 1 tsp anardana (pomegranate seeds)
- 1/2 tsp rock salt
- pinch asafoetida
- 1 tsp kasoori methi
- 1 tsp amchur powder
- 3 tsp Everest chhole/chana masala blend (author's specific brand recommendation after testing 5 brands)

TEMPERING:
- 2 tbsp ghee
- 3 garlic cloves, finely chopped
- 1 tsp kashmiri red chili powder
- 1 tsp cumin
- 10-12 thinly sliced ginger juliennes

PREP:
- Boil the onions in water for 3-4 min BEFORE pureeing — pureeing raw onions gives a bitter taste; boiling first fixes this.
- Puree the tomatoes (blanching and skinning optional).
- Grind ginger and garlic into a paste with water; chop green chilies separately (or include in the paste).

COOK THE BEANS:
1. Soak the beans overnight.
2. In a pressure cooker, add 3-4 cups water + baking soda.
3. Tie tea, whole cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon in a small cloth bundle; add bay leaves separately.
4. Pressure cook beans for 3-4 whistles until soft. (Canned beans: skip baking soda, 1 whistle just to infuse.)
5. SEPARATE BEANS FROM STOCK — DO NOT THROW THE STOCK AWAY. The stock is essential.

GRAVY:
1. Heat oil/ghee in a pan.
2. Add pureed boiled onions, cook on medium / medium-high heat for 15-20 min (or more) until light brown, stirring occasionally.
3. Add ginger-garlic paste and chilies; cook a couple of minutes.
4. Add pureed tomatoes; cook on medium / medium-high for 15 min, stirring occasionally.
5. Add the ground dry spice mix; cook 3-4 min.
6. Add the beans, mix, cook a couple of minutes (subtle flavor develops here before stock goes in).
7. Add the reserved stock + salt; simmer 5-7 min.

TEMPERING (OPTIONAL — author says it elevates the dish):
1. Heat ghee in a small frying/tempering pan.
2. Add chopped garlic, cumin, ginger juliennes; fry briefly (don't brown).
3. Stir in kashmiri red chili powder.
4. Pour the tempering mixture carefully over the prepared chana masala.

FINISH: sprinkle cilantro on top.

THINGS WORTH LEARNING FROM THIS RECIPE:
- The bean-infusion step (cooking the beans in tea + whole spices + baking soda, then reserving the spiced stock for the gravy).
- The boil-the-onions-before-pureeing technique to avoid raw-onion bitterness.
- The double-spice approach (custom mix + packaged Everest chana masala blend) — possibly worth testing whether the redundancy is real.
- The closing tempering step (kashmiri chili in ghee with garlic, cumin, ginger juliennes, poured over at the end).
- 15-20 min onion cook AND 15 min tomato cook — substantially longer than most chana masala recipes call for.
